resources.string: PowerBridge will walk you through PC setup first, then help you choose the wake method that fits your home.
1. Scan the QR code from PowerBridge Windows Companion.\n2. Confirm your PC details.\n3. Choose how you want wake to work.
Wake and boot your PC with simple setup choices for home Wi-Fi or remote wake.
Choose Setup Help if you are not sure which wake path fits your setup. Diagnostics history stays available.
Action in progress. Open Diagnostics for step-by-step detail.
Running a setup or connection check for %1$s.
Clear local checks and a copyable debug log for support.
Run diagnostics or copy the current log when you need help troubleshooting.
Full report ZIP opened in the Android share sheet.
No diagnostic events recorded yet.\n\nRun Diagnostics or use Wake, Boot, or Check Method first.
Scan your PC first, confirm the details, then choose the wake path that fits your home.
Step 1: Scan Your PC Setup QR
Open PowerBridge Windows Companion on your PC, generate the QR code, then scan it here.
Your PC details are loaded. Next, choose how you want wake to work.
Waiting for your PC setup QR.
PC setup confirmed. Now choose where you want wake to work.
Step 2: Where do you want Wake to work?
Start with the simple answer first. PowerBridge will only show the more advanced remote choices if you need them.
Best when your phone and PC are on the same home Wi-Fi.
Use this when you want wake to work over cellular or when you are away from your house.
PowerBridge will point you toward the easiest starting path.
Step 3: What can you leave powered on at home?
Choose the helper you already have at home. Planned paths stay marked clearly so they do not look ready before they are.
Use a spare Android phone or tablet left plugged in at home.
Use a streaming device or Android TV left on at home.
Use a smart speaker setup such as Alexa or Google Home.
Force the PC to boot by power-cycling a smart plug or similar hardware path.
Use your own NAS, Raspberry Pi, router, server, or Docker host.
This is the best starting point when your phone and PC are on the same home Wi-Fi. It works now and keeps setup simple.
Ready now. Your imported PC looks usable for same-home Wi-Fi wake.
Needs setup. PowerBridge still needs complete PC wake details before Home Wi-Fi Wake is ready.
Use an Old Phone or Tablet at Home
Use an old Android phone or tablet that stays plugged in at home. This is the easiest future remote option for most people once relay pairing is finished.
Prototype. Home Relay Mode exists in the app, but real remote relay runtime is not finished yet.
Use a Fire TV or Smart TV at Home
Use a Fire TV Stick, Chromecast with Google TV, or Android TV device left on at home as the wake helper.
Coming later. Good fit for homes that already keep a TV device powered on.
Use a voice or smart home setup, such as Alexa or Google Home, instead of a spare phone.
Coming later. This is still a research path in PowerBridge.
Use a Smart Plug or Hardware Power Path
Use a smart plug or similar power path to force the PC to turn on. This is a hardware workaround, not standard Wake-on-LAN.
Coming later. This depends on your motherboard and hardware power settings.
Use your own relay server, NAS, Raspberry Pi, Docker host, router, or home server. This is the advanced self-hosted path already tied to Home Relay Server in the app.
Advanced. Available now if you already run your own server path and finish the required settings.
Best starting point: Home Wi-Fi first

If you only need wake at home, choose Home Wi-Fi only. If you want wake away from home and can leave an old phone or tablet plugged in, use Old Phone / Tablet Relay later. If you already run a NAS, Raspberry Pi, or server, choose My Own Server. The other options are planned for later.
Start simple. Home Wi-Fi Wake is the safest first choice for most people.
Scan and confirm your PC setup first.
Setup Guide cancelled the QR scan.
Setup Guide could not read that QR code.
Method chosen. You can now return to the main screen and continue setup when needed.
Use Wake PC from the main screen when %1$s and this phone are on the same home Wi-Fi.
Open Settings for %1$s only if you still need to finish local PC details such as MAC address or broadcast IP.
Your advanced server path looks configured. Test it from the main screen or run Diagnostics if you want to verify it.
This advanced path is available, but %1$s still needs your relay URL, token, or other server details in Settings before it is ready.
PowerBridge set %1$s to Home Wi-Fi Wake because it is the simplest live path in this build.
PowerBridge set %1$s to Home Wi-Fi Wake as the easiest starting path. Finish any missing PC details in Settings if needed.
This path is still a prototype. Use Home Wi-Fi Wake now, or keep watching for a later PowerBridge phase that finishes old-phone relay pairing.
This path is planned for later. Use Home Wi-Fi Wake now, or use My Own Server if you already run a home server.
This path is still research only. Use Home Wi-Fi Wake now, or My Own Server if you already have self-hosted gear.
This path is planned for later and depends on hardware behavior. Use Home Wi-Fi Wake now unless you already need an advanced self-hosted method.
Use this same PowerBridge app on a spare Android phone or tablet that stays plugged in at home.
Home Relay Mode is the future old phone or tablet path for remote wake. Pairing and live remote wake are not finished in this build yet.
Choose a wake style and fill in only the settings that path needs.
This removes the saved wake setup for %1$s. PowerBridge will switch to another remaining profile.
Import failed. Review the JSON and try again.
Profile created from Windows Companion. Active PC is now %1$s.
Profile updated from Windows Companion. Active PC is now %1$s.
Scan the QR code shown by PowerBridge Windows Companion. This prepares a Local Wi-Fi Wake profile from your PC.
At least one profile must remain.
PowerBridge can download a newer APK from GitHub Releases and hand it to the Android installer. Android still requires your confirmation before replacing the app.
Target PC IP for status checks
Use this when the PC is sleeping and you want it to resume.
Use this for full power-on attempts when the target hardware supports wake from shutdown.
PowerBridge v1 supports wake and boot only. Shutdown and restart require a separate target-side backend or agent.
Can support cellular with a home anchor: Yes
Can support cellular with a home anchor: No

Home Relay Server is live in this build for advanced self-hosted setups. Use it only if you already have your own reachable relay, server, NAS, Raspberry Pi, router, or Docker host. Do not expose raw Wake-on-LAN UDP ports directly to the public internet.

Local Wi-Fi Wake is live in this build. Best first step: tap Auto-detect local network, then enter your PC MAC. This method works only while the phone is on the same Wi-Fi or LAN as the target PC. Ethernet is recommended for the most reliable Wake-on-LAN support, and Wi-Fi wake from shutdown is often hardware-dependent.

Home Device Relay is coming later. It is planned for an always-on phone, tablet, Fire TV, Android TV, or Google TV device left at home to receive a secure request and send the local Wake-on-LAN packet.

Smart Plug Boot Assist is coming later. It is a hardware workaround, not standard Wake-on-LAN. It will require BIOS or UEFI Restore on AC Power Loss support, and power cycling should be used carefully.
Advanced Network Setup is guides-later content for users who already run router Wake-on-LAN, VPN access, or other custom network paths. It is not a live guided setup in this build.
Smart Home Wake is currently research only. PowerBridge does not yet implement Alexa, Google Home, Home Assistant, or other smart-home wake integrations in this build.
Detected values are suggestions. Review before saving.
Ready for Local Wi-Fi setup check after saving.
Connect this phone to the same Wi-Fi as your PC, then try again.
Detected local settings applied. Review them, then press Save.
Switch Wake Method to Local Wi-Fi Wake before using Auto-detect local network.
